content.thinking:
Ah interesting! The actual files on disk use `OrderStateMachine.ts` and `OrderStateMachine.test.ts` (capital O). But earlier when I ran `ls`, they showed as `orderStateMachine.test.ts` and `orderStateMachine.ts` (lowercase). This is confusing. Let me check again.

Wait, looking at the `ls` output from my first bash command, it shows:
```
orderStateMachine.test.ts


orderStateMachine.ts
```

But when I `ls /Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/order-state-machine/`, it shows:
```
OrderStateMachine.test.ts
OrderStateMachine.ts
```

So the files inside the `order-state-machine` subdirectory use capital O, and the files at the `.tmp` root level also exist but with lowercase. So my reads were of the lowercase versions.

Wait, actually the first `ls` I ran showed the content of `.tmp`, not `.tmp/order-state-machine/`. Looking at the output again, those lowercase files are at the root level of `.tmp`, while the capitalized versions are in the subdirectory.

Since macOS has case-insensitive filesystems by default, both paths resolve to the same file anyway. Let me just run the tests and see what happens.

content.text: Files exist with capital O. The content I read matches - macOS case-insensitive FS. Now let me run the tests to see their current state.
